mission.title: Install and Configure Containerssh on Kubernetes
mission.description: ContainerSSH is an SSH server that launches containers on demand in Kubernetes and Docker, providing dynamic access for various use cases such as lab environments, debugging, and honeypots. Installing ContainerSSH allows you to manage containerized environments efficiently and securely.

steps.title: Add the ContainerSSH Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official ContainerSSH Helm repository to your Helm configuration. This allows you to install ContainerSSH using Helm.
```bash
helm repo add containerssh https://containerssh.github.io/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install ContainerSSH using Helm
steps.description:
Install ContainerSSH with a specific version and create a namespace for it.
```bash
helm install containerssh containerssh/containerssh --version v0.5.2 --namespace containerssh --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the ContainerSSH pods are running successfully in the namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n containerssh
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Edit the deployment to set resource limits for the ContainerSSH pods.
```bash
kubectl edit deployment containerssh -n containerssh
# Add the following under spec.containers:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     cpu: "500m"
#     memory: "512Mi"
```

steps.title: Set up port forwarding for access
steps.description:
Forward a local port to the ContainerSSH service to access it.
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/containerssh -n containerssh 2222:22
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the ContainerSSH Helm release to remove the application from your cluster.
```bash
helm uninstall containerssh --namespace containerssh
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and resources
uninstall.description:
Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and other resources that were created during the installation.
```bash
kubectl delete crd containersshs.containerssh.io
```

uninstall.title: Remove the namespace
uninstall.description:
Delete the namespace used for ContainerSSH to clean up any remaining resources.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace containerssh
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, backup the current configuration and state of ContainerSSH.
```bash
kubectl get all -n containerssh -o yaml > containerssh-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository
upgrade.description:
Ensure you have the latest version of the ContainerSSH Helm chart.
```bash
helm repo update
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade ContainerSSH
upgrade.description:
Upgrade the ContainerSSH installation to the latest version.
```bash
helm upgrade containerssh containerssh/containerssh --version v0.5.2 --namespace containerssh
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check the status of the pods to ensure they are running the new version.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n containerssh
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in Pending state
troubleshooting.description:
This may indicate insufficient resources in the cluster. Check the events for the pods to diagnose.
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n containerssh
```

troubleshooting.title: ContainerSSH service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
Ensure that the port forwarding is set up correctly and the service is running. Check the service status.
```bash
kubectl get svc -n containerssh
```

troubleshooting.title: Authentication failures
troubleshooting.description:
Check the logs of the ContainerSSH pod for any authentication-related errors.
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n containerssh
```

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing OOMKilled
troubleshooting.description:
If pods are being killed due to out-of-memory errors, consider increasing the memory limits in the deployment.
```bash
kubectl edit deployment containerssh -n containerssh
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of ContainerSSH will have all pods running in the 'containerssh' namespace, and you will be able to SSH into the service using the forwarded port. You can also verify that resource limits are correctly configured.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add containerssh https://containerssh.github.io/charts
helm repo update
helm install containerssh containerssh/containerssh --version v0.5.2 --namespace containerssh --create-namespace

kubectl edit deployment containerssh -n containerssh
# Add the following under spec.containers:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     cpu: "500m"
#     memory: "512Mi"

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and both Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
